page.title: Project work with more context, process, and outcome.
page.description: Featured projects stay at the top, with the rest of the portfolio available below through the expanded archive and filters.
page.searchPlaceholder: Search by title, tool, or tag
page.emptyMessage: No projects match the current search.

projects.title: Solar Farm GIS Project
projects.imageAlt: Solar Farm GIS Project map output
projects.summary: Five-part GIS coursework project identifying candidate solar farm sites on Saddle Lake IR #125 through layered feasibility analysis and cost-per-MWh comparison.
projects.impact: Reduced the full reserve area to four candidate parcels with quantified capacity, annual generation, and infrastructure access cost. Parcel 1 ranked most economical (CostPerMWh 0.196); Parcel 2 offered the largest potential output at 71.9 ha and ~485,000 MWh per year.
projects.role: Spatial analysis, suitability modelling, cartographic output
projects.overview: A five-part coursework project (GEOG 2551/2553) applying layered GIS suitability analysis to Saddle Lake Indian Reserve #125, approximately 130 km northeast of Edmonton. Each assignment built on the last, culminating in a ranked shortlist of four candidate solar farm sites.
projects.challenge: The core challenge was moving from a broad study area to a defendable shortlist of candidate sites while keeping the tradeoffs between legal, economic, and technical constraints visible and comparable.
process: Preprocessed environmental constraint layers (Wildlife Habitat Sensitivity Risk, roads, lakes, streams) and a DEM-derived slope and aspect surface to define exclusion zones and suitable terrain.
Built a combined road and transmission line access cost surface using distance accumulation to estimate infrastructure cost at every cell in the study area.

Intersected legal, economic, and technical feasibility layers to produce a 'Truly Potential Land' layer, then drew four candidate parcels and calculated installed capacity, annual generation, and CostPerMWh for each.
projects.outcome: Four candidate parcels with quantified attributes: Parcel 1 was the most economical site (CostPerMWh 0.196, access cost ~$54,000); Parcel 2 offered the highest output (71.9 ha, ~485,000 MWh/year) at moderate cost.

detailSections.title: What this project was
detailSections.body: A five-part GIS coursework project applying sequential suitability analysis to a real study area - translating environmental, economic, and terrain constraints into a ranked set of candidate solar sites.

detailSections.title: How it was done
detailSections.body: Each assignment contributed a layer to the final result: constraint mapping, terrain derivatives, infrastructure cost surfaces, feasibility intersection, and candidate site creation with CostPerMWh comparison.

detailSections.title: Why it mattered
detailSections.body: The workflow produced a shortlist that could support a real planning discussion - showing how location relative to roads and transmission lines dominated site economics once all constraints were combined.

projects.title: Parks & Pathways Overview
projects.imageAlt: Interactive map showing Calgary parks and pathways layers
projects.summary: Interactive Leaflet map exploring Calgary's green-space network with toggleable parks, trails, and boundary layers.
projects.impact: Turned a course assignment web map into a more useful exploratory interface - going beyond the minimum requirements to add layer controls, filtering, and reset behavior that let viewers compare Calgary's green-space network on their own terms.
projects.role: Web mapping, interface design, GIS communication
projects.overview: Built a responsive web map that helps users visually compare how Calgary parks and pathway corridors relate to one another across the city.
projects.challenge: The goal was to make geographic relationships easier to inspect without overwhelming the user with a heavy GIS-style interface.
process: Loaded parks and trail layers from ArcGIS services and combined them with a local Calgary boundary dataset.
Added layer controls, reset behaviour, and simple filtering so the map could move beyond a static display.
Adjusted the interface to support both desktop and smaller screens while keeping the controls readable.
projects.outcome: Users can toggle layers, filter the visible data, and identify corridors where major park areas appear to connect through the pathway network.

detailSections.title: What this project was
detailSections.body: A front-end web-mapping project designed to communicate spatial relationships clearly through an interactive browser-based map.

detailSections.title: How it was done
detailSections.body: The map uses Leaflet for rendering, Esri-Leaflet for hosted data access, and a lightweight control panel for filtering and view management.

detailSections.title: Why it mattered
detailSections.body: It demonstrates both GIS thinking and practical interface decisions: not just loading spatial data, but shaping how people explore it.

projects.title: Web Games Lab
projects.imageAlt: Illustrated preview card for the Web Games Lab page
projects.summary: Small browser-based games and interactive tools built to make colour reading, logic, and data intuition more hands-on.
projects.impact: Creates a dedicated home for browser-based experiments that would otherwise sit as loose files with no context. Current builds include a two-player Dodgeball game rebuilt from the first game ever programmed (originally in Lua) and an RGB Guesser for building colour-reading intuition.
projects.role: Game design, front-end development, interactive learning
projects.overview: A lightweight page for small browser-based experiments and mini-games that can grow over time without crowding the main portfolio.
projects.challenge: The goal is to keep these projects playful and approachable while still making them feel consistent with the rest of the site.
process: Created a hidden landing page outside the main navigation so the games can live as a side branch of the portfolio rather than competing with the main site structure.
Set up a separate data file and renderer so new games can be added later by extending a list instead of rebuilding the page each time.
Built the first game as a standalone page so each game can have its own rules, styling, and interaction logic without turning one page into a tangle of conditionals.
projects.outcome: The portfolio now has a dedicated Web Games page and a first playable RGB guessing game, with room to expand later.

detailSections.title: Why this section exists
detailSections.body: It gives smaller experimental builds a clear home. That makes it easier to add short interactive pieces that support learning, visual thinking, or technical experimentation.

projects.title: Video Game Patch Analysis
projects.summary: Analyzed Valorant patch 11.08 to quantify whether balance changes shifted agent pick rates and win rates across ranked brackets and professional play.
projects.impact: Identified the sentinel role had dropped below a 20% pick rate - a threshold argued to represent roughly one sentinel per team on average given four roles and five players - and built a structured before/after comparison to support the interpretation rather than relying on community perception.
projects.overview: Treated Valorant patch 11.08 as a structured analysis problem: collect pre- and post-patch agent and role balance data, clean it, compare it across ranked brackets and pro play, and interpret whether the patch actually changed the game's balance in a meaningful way.
projects.challenge: Patch discussion in the Valorant community is typically driven by perception rather than evidence. The goal was to build a before/after comparison that could test whether role viability was genuinely shifting.
process: Collected and cleaned agent pick rate and win rate data across ranked brackets and professional play for pre- and post-patch periods.
Analyzed balance at the role level - duelist, initiator, controller, sentinel - to surface patterns not visible in per-agent numbers alone.
Built visualizations showing where key indicators shifted and framed the sentinel class drop below 20% pick rate as a measurable balance signal, using team composition logic as a reference point.
projects.outcome: Produced a structured analysis showing the sentinel class had dropped below the 20% pick rate threshold post-patch, with supporting evidence across ranked and professional contexts.

detailSections.title: What this project was
detailSections.body: A data analysis project applying structured before/after comparison to Valorant patch 11.08, focused on agent and role balance across competitive play tiers.

detailSections.title: How it was done
detailSections.body: Pick rate and win rate data were collected, cleaned, and analyzed by agent and role across ranked brackets and pro play, with results visualized to surface patterns not obvious from community discussion alone.

detailSections.title: Why it mattered
detailSections.body: It demonstrates practical analysis habits: define the question, structure the data, visualize the signal, and explain the result with a clear and defensible reference point rather than relying on anecdote.

projects.title: Reinforcement Learning Lab
projects.summary: Seven trained RL agents from the Hugging Face Deep RL Course, covering tabular Q-learning, REINFORCE, DQN, PPO, and Unity ML-Agents across classic control, Atari, and continuous environments.
projects.impact: Completed the full Hugging Face Deep RL Course with trained and evaluated agents across five algorithm families, each published to the Hub for reuse and review.
projects.role: Model training, evaluation, experiment tracking, Hugging Face Hub publishing
projects.tools: Python, Stable-Baselines3, Gymnasium, Unity ML-Agents, NumPy, Matplotlib, Hugging Face Hub
projects.overview: A collection of trained reinforcement learning agents published to the Hugging Face Hub, built through the Hugging Face Deep RL Course. The course progressed from tabular Q-learning through policy gradients (REINFORCE), deep Q-networks (DQN), PPO with parallel environments, and Unity ML-Agents.
projects.challenge: Each agent required its own training configuration, environment setup, and evaluation workflow. The goal was to produce trained, evaluated, and packaged agents rather than one-off notebooks.
process: Trained tabular Q-learning agents on FrozenLake-v1 (no-slip) and Taxi-v3 using explicit Q-tables in small discrete environments.
Applied REINFORCE (policy gradient) to CartPole-v1 and PixelCopter, learning to sample and update policies directly from episode returns.
Trained a DQN agent on SpaceInvadersNoFrameskip-v4 and a PPO agent on LunarLander-v3 (302.83 mean reward) using parallel environments and evaluation callbacks.
Trained a Huggy agent using Unity ML-Agents PPO in a custom continuous-action environment.
projects.outcome: Seven trained agents across five algorithm families, each evaluated and published to Hugging Face Hub.

detailSections.title: What this project was
detailSections.body: A structured progression through the Hugging Face Deep RL Course - from tabular methods to deep networks to continuous control - with each agent trained, evaluated, and published.

detailSections.title: How it was done
detailSections.body: Each agent used the appropriate library and training loop for its algorithm family: Q-tables for tabular methods, PyTorch policy networks for REINFORCE, Stable-Baselines3 for PPO and DQN, and Unity ML-Agents for Huggy.

detailSections.title: Why it mattered
detailSections.body: It demonstrates breadth across RL algorithm families and the discipline to follow a structured training and evaluation workflow from start to finish.

projects.title: White Tiger Websites
projects.summary: Responsive multi-page website with reusable layout components and interactive forms.
projects.impact: Demonstrated a complete small-site build with reusable layout patterns, responsive behavior, and form logic rather than isolated page mockups.
projects.role: Front-end development, responsive layout, form behavior
projects.overview: Built a fictional agency website with multiple pages, shared layout components, a pricing/quotes page, and a separate feedback flow.
projects.challenge: The project needed to feel consistent across pages while still supporting page-specific content and interactions.
process: Created a shared structure for navigation, page framing, and footer components across the site.
Used responsive layout techniques so the design adapted across screen sizes.
Added interactive form behavior, including conditionally displayed inputs, to make the pages feel more complete.
projects.outcome: Produced a cohesive site that combined reusable structure with responsive behavior and working user-input patterns.

detailSections.title: What this project was
detailSections.body: A front-end web project focused on consistency, responsiveness, and the practical structure of a small multi-page site.

detailSections.title: How it was done
detailSections.body: The build relied on reusable layout pieces, Bootstrap-assisted structure, and CSS/media-query refinement.

detailSections.title: Why it mattered
detailSections.body: It shows the difference between building a single page and building a site that has to stay coherent across multiple user paths.

projects.title: Procedural Tank Artillery Game
projects.imageAlt: Projectile trajectories over procedural terrain in a tank artillery game
projects.summary: Turn-based artillery game with procedural terrain, projectile physics, and a simple computer opponent.
projects.impact: Combined math, physics, and simulation logic into a playable terminal game that visualized each turn and tracked state over time.
projects.role: Simulation logic, procedural generation, gameplay systems
projects.overview: Implemented a turn-based artillery game in Python where terrain is procedurally generated and each shot is simulated against the landscape.
projects.challenge: The core challenge was combining generation, collision checks, and game-state updates in a way that remained understandable and playable.
process: Generated terrain using randomized sine-based parameters to produce different maps from round to round.
Simulated projectile paths and checked for collisions against the terrain profile.
Tracked tank health and turn outcomes, visualizing the state after each action.
projects.outcome: Created a complete playable loop with procedural terrain, plotted projectiles, and win/loss state tracking.

detailSections.title: What this project was
detailSections.body: A programming and simulation exercise that turned mathematical ideas into a small but complete playable system.

detailSections.title: How it was done
detailSections.body: The project combined procedural terrain creation, per-turn simulation, and terminal-driven game flow.

detailSections.title: Why it mattered
detailSections.body: It shows comfort with implementing logic from scratch and translating technical rules into a working experience.
